15: archegos tes zoes (archēgon tēs zōēs): archēgos means pioneer, founder, or champion — one who blazes a trail others follow. In Hebrews 2:10 and Acts 12:2, Christ is archēgos of salvation and faith. Peter's phrase is therefore not merely 'author of life' (as if life were a text Jesus wrote) but 'pioneer of life' — the one who broke through death to open the way. The audience had killed the pioneer of life; God reversed the verdict by resurrection. The irony is Luke's sharpest Christological paradox in Acts.
22: propheten ... hos eme (prophētēn ... hōs eme): Peter quotes Deuteronomy 18:15 and links it to Jesus. The 'prophet like Moses' in Deuteronomic expectation was to bring a new word from YHWH with authority like the Sinai revelation. The rabbinic tradition expected this prophet at the eschaton (1QS Acts 9:11 — the Qumran community awaited the prophet along with the Messiah). Peter claims the eschatological prophet has arrived: the new Moses not merely like him but surpassing him.
1: tēn hōran tēs proseuchēs tēn enatēn (tēn hōran tēs proseuchēs tēn enatēn, 'the hour of prayer, the ninth hour'): 3 p.m., the hour of the minchah offering and one of the three daily prayer times (Psalms 55:17). The miracle happens within Israel's structured worship life, not outside it; Peter and John are observant Jews who continue temple practice. The crucifixion was at the sixth hour (noon), the darkness lasted until the ninth hour (Luke 23:44-46) — the hour of prayer is also the hour of Christ's death.
2: pros tēn thyran tou hierou tēn legomenēn Hōraian (pros tēn thyran tou hierou tēn legomenēn Hōraian, 'at the temple gate called Beautiful'): the gate's identity is disputed (possibly the Nicanor Gate or the Eastern Gate); Hōraia (beautiful) may preserve a punning connection with the healing — hōra (hour, right time) as well as beauty. Beggars positioned themselves where worshippers would feel obligated to give; the gate threshold was the liminal space between the clean and the common.
3: eleēmosynēn labein (eleēmosynēn labein, 'to receive alms'): eleēmosyne is 'merciful giving', derived from eleos (mercy/lovingkindness = chesed). Almsgiving at the temple gate was a standard piety practice (Tob Acts 4:7-11; Sir Acts 35:22); the man's request is within the normal social expectation. What he receives exceeds what he asked by the kind of ratio that characterizes the kingdom economy throughout Luke-Acts.
4: atenisas de Petros eis auton (atenisas de Petros eis auton, 'having fixed his gaze on him'): atenizō (to gaze fixedly) is a Lukan characteristic (Acts 1:10; Acts 3:12; Acts 6:15; Acts 7:55; Acts 10:4; Acts 11:6; Acts 13:9; Acts 14:9); it indicates the focused attention that precedes a significant act or revelation. Peter's 'look at us' (blepson eis hēmas) is a command to the beggar to attend — to shift from peripheral passivity to full engagement before the gift is given.
5: epeiches autois prosdokōn ti par' autōn labein (epeiches autois prosdokōn ti par' autōn labein, 'he gave them attention, expecting to receive something from them'): prosdokaō (expect, await) is the same word used for eschatological expectation in Luke-Acts. The man's expectation is perfectly reasonable and even necessary to the miracle — he must look up and receive. What he will receive is something other than what he expects; the kingdom repeatedly delivers the unexpected within genuine expectation.
6: argyrion kai chrysion ouch hyparchei moi (argyrion kai chrysion ouch hyparchei moi, 'silver and gold do not belong to me'): Peter's declaration is not poverty-complaint but Christological redirection; what he has is incomparably greater than what was asked. en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou ('in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth'): the healing is not Peter's own power but the authority invested in the name — the name that carries the person, character, and power of the risen Christ.
7: ai baseis autou kai ta sphydra estereothesan (hai baseis autou kai ta sphydra estereōthēsan, 'his feet and ankles were made firm/strong'): Luke's medical precision — baseis (feet/soles) and sphydra (ankles) are the specific anatomical terms for the parts that had been congenitally weak. Stereōō (make firm/solid) is used for the spiritual strengthening of faith (Acts 16:5) and here for physical restoration; the solidity given to the ankles mirrors the spiritual solidity that characterizes the healed community.
8: exallomenos estē kai periepatei kai eisēlthen syn autois eis to hieron peripatōn kai hallomenos (exallomenos estē kai periepatei, 'leaping up he stood and walked'): the verbs pile up in an exuberant verbal sequence — leaping, standing, walking, entering, walking, leaping, praising. The man who had never walked is now celebrating in the very temple from which his condition had excluded him from full participation. The healing is simultaneously physical restoration and social-religious reintegration.
9: eidon pantes hoi laoi auton peripatounta kai ainounta ton theon ('all the people saw him walking and praising God'): the witnessing community (pantes hoi laoi) is the necessary context for the miracle's sign-function. The healing is not a private benefit but a public declaration; the praising (ainounta) directs the crowd's attention to God, not to Peter. Luke's consistent pattern: signs point beyond themselves to the God who acts through human agents.
10: plēsthēsan thambous kai ekstaselōs (eplēsthēsan thambous kai ekstaselōs, 'they were filled with amazement and wonder'): thampos (amazement) and ekstasis (astonishment, being outside oneself) together describe the overwhelmed response to the unexpected divine act. The same man they had seen begging at the Beautiful Gate is now walking — the specificity of the recognition ('this is the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate') intensifies the miracle's impact: the sign is verifiable.
11: Stoa Solomōntos (Stoā Solomōntos, 'Solomon's Colonnade'): the eastern portico of the temple precincts, mentioned also in John 10:23; it became the preferred location for the Jerusalem church's public teaching (Acts 5:12). The colonnade's association with Solomon evokes the Davidic-temple tradition; the church teaching under Solomon's Colonnade is the community of the new covenant gathered in the place that symbolized David's dynastic promise.
12: ti atenidzete hēmin (ti atenidzete hēmin, 'why do you stare at us'): Peter's first words deflect the crowd's attention from the apostles to God; the same deflection recurs when the crowd at Lystra tries to worship Paul and Barnabas (Acts 14:14-15). idia dynamei ē eusebeia ('our own power or piety'): dynamis (power) and eusebeia (piety/godliness) are the two things Peter explicitly denies as the source — rejecting both the thaumaturgical and the meritorious explanations.
13: ho theos Abraam kai Isaak kai Iakōb ('the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob'): Peter's sermon opens with the Exodus formula (Exodus 3:6, 15; Acts 4:5) — the God who speaks in Pentecost is the same covenant God who heard Israel in Egypt. pais (pais, 'servant/child'): the ambiguous Greek term encompasses both 'servant' (LXX Isa 52-53) and 'child/son'; the Isaianic servant-connotation is primary but the sonship nuance is present. Peter uses pais four times in Acts 3-4 as his primary Christological category.
14: ton hagion kai dikaion (ton hagion kai dikaion, 'the Holy and Righteous One'): both are divine-quality terms applied to Jesus. Hagios (holy, set apart for God) was the demon's identification at Capernaum (Luke 4:34) and Gabriel's announcement (Luke 1:35); dikaios (righteous) evokes Isaiah 53:11's righteous Servant. The demand to release a murderer (andra phonea) in exchange for the Holy and Righteous One is the ultimate moral paradox of the trial.
16: epi tē pistei tou onomatos autou (epi tē pistei tou onomatos autou, 'on the faith of his name'): the grammatical syntax is compressed — it is debated whether the faith is the man's faith in Jesus, or the faith that Jesus's name itself exercises. Both readings have exegetical defenders; the most natural reading is that the faith-channel (epi tē pistei) through which the name's power flows includes the healed man's own response. Holoklaesia (complete soundness) is a hapax legomenon — total restoration, nothing lacking.
17: oid' hoti kata agnoian epraxate (oid' hoti kata agnoian epraxate, 'I know that in ignorance you acted'): agnoia (ignorance) is a mitigating category in both Jewish and Roman law. Peter's attribution is pastoral: the audience present at the sermon is not the Sanhedrin that deliberated but the crowd that followed the wave of religious pressure. The Leviticus 4:2/Numbers 15:22-29 distinction between sins of ignorance and deliberate rebellion underlies the appeal — ignorance opens the door to repentance that deliberate sin would close.
18: prokatanggeilas dia stomatos pantōn tōn prophētōn pathein ton Christon autou (prokatanggeilas dia stomatos pantōn tōn prophētōn pathein ton Christon autou, 'having foretold through the mouth of all his prophets that his Messiah would suffer'): the universal prophetic witness to a suffering Messiah is Peter's interpretive claim — all the prophets collectively pointed to the passion. The MKT's 'all his prophets' (dia stomatos pantōn) emphasizes comprehensiveness; no single prophet is named because the whole canonical trajectory, not a single text, is the ground of the claim.
19: exaleiphthēnai hymōn hai hamartiai (exaleiphthēnai hymōn hai hamartiai, 'your sins may be wiped out'): exaleiphō (blot out, erase) is the vocabulary of erasing a written record — the metaphor of sin as debt that is cancelled. The same word in Colossians 2:14 for the 'certificate of debt' nailed to the cross. kairoi anapsyxeōs (kairoi anapsyxeōs, 'times of refreshing'): anapsyxis (refreshment, respite) is a rare word; it describes the cooling relief of shade in the heat (LXX Exodus 8:15). The messianic era is framed as eschatological refreshment — relief from the weight of the old order.
20: ton prokecheiri smenon hymin Christon Iēsoun (ton prokecheirismo menon hymin Christon Iēsoun, 'the Messiah appointed for you — even Jesus'): procheirizomai (appoint beforehand, designate) implies prior divine action; the Messiah was designated for Israel in particular. The syntax is striking: 'send the Christ, who has been designated for you, even Jesus' — the definite article and the personal name confirm that the 'designated Messiah' and 'Jesus of Nazareth' are identical.
21: apokatastaseōs pantōn (apokatastaseōs pantōn, 'restoration of all things'): apokatastasis appears only here in the NT but was an important eschatological category in Jewish thought (Malachi 4:6; Isaiah 65:17-25; Ezek 36-37). The cosmic scope ('all things') goes beyond mere national restoration to include the new creation. The heaven's must receive (dei ... ouranou ... dexasthai) Christ until this restoration — ascension and parousia are the brackets of the eschatological interval in which the church lives and witnesses.
23: eksolethreuthēsetai ek tou laou (eksoleuthreuthēsetai ek tou laou, 'will be utterly destroyed from the people'): the verb combines ex- (completeness) with olothreuo (destroy) for total exclusion — the LXX's intensified karet penalty (Leviticus 23:29). Peter applies the Deuteronomic prophet-rejection penalty (Deuteronomy 18:19) to refusal of Jesus; to reject the prophet-like-Moses is to put oneself outside the covenant community.
24: apo Samouēl kai tōn kathexēs (apo Samouēl kai tōn kathexēs, 'from Samuel and those following in order'): Samuel is chosen as the first in the sequence of prophets who 'foretold these days'; he is the first writing-adjacent prophet to speak specifically about David and the Davidic dynasty. The kathexēs (in order, successively) implies a continuous prophetic chain from Samuel through the Major and Minor Prophets — all pointing toward the Christ who has now arrived.
25: huioi tōn prophētōn kai tēs diathēkēs (huioi tōn prophētōn kai tēs diathēkēs, 'sons of the prophets and of the covenant'): the double huioi (sons) is a Semitic idiom for members of a group or inheritors of a tradition. The audience stands within the prophetic inheritance (they are heirs of the Scriptures Peter has been citing) and within the Abrahamic covenant (diathēkē here is the covenant of Genesis 12:3; Acts 22:18). The gospel comes 'to you first' (Acts 3:26) because the covenant priority is Israel's.
26: anastēsas ho theos ton paida autou (anastēsas ho theos ton paida autou, 'God having raised up his servant'): anastēsas (having raised up) can refer either to resurrection or to the OT idiom 'raise up a prophet' (Deuteronomy 18:15, 18); the ambiguity may be intentional — both senses are present. Apestellen (sent): the Servant is sent specifically to bless Israel first (protōn), by turning each person from wickedness. Repentance is the specific content of the blessing.
